latest.verdict:
Drawing the threads · the convocation converges on a single target file with a small, important disagreement about which line inside it to touch first. The highest-conviction next move is this: open umbris-core/tests/test_introspection.py and read it end to end before any new test file is created for the thirty-two flagged modules. Inside that read, perform two ordered acts.

First · locate the severity-scoring function in the introspection scanner itself (somewhere under umbris-core/src/umbris/, not in the test file · Mars is correct that no planet has yet named the producing module, so step zero is to grep for the sev= emission site, plausibly in provenance.py, umbra.py, or a dedicated introspection module). Read lines 83, 93, 101, 102, and 284 of test_introspection.py in their actual context. This single read resolves the Venus/Mars dispute empirically: either the FIXME at line 93 indicts the scanner's ranker (Venus's read) or it marks an unwritten test assertion against a ranker that is already correct (Mars's read). The current scan data is weakly consistent with Mars · XXX at sev=4 outranks FIXME at sev=3, which is a plausible ordering · but FIXME and TODO both appearing at sev=3 in this very report is the inversion Venus's hypothesis predicts, so the question is live, not settled.

Second · execute in this order. (a) If the ranker is wrong, fix it in the scanner module and only then write the assertion at line 93 that pins the corrected behaviour · Mars's repair clause, adopted. (b) Execute the sev=4 rewrite at line 102 and the sev=3 critical FIXME at line 284 as a pair, since they are the two highest-severity items in the entire scan and likely share context. (c) Commit with a note that the introspection baseline is now trustworthy. Only after this commit do the thirty-two test_gap items become tractable, and they should then be addressed not one-by-one but by generating a test scaffold pattern · the gap is systemic (one missing test file per source module across the entire tree), so the second move after this one is a single scaffolding pass, not thirty-two commits.

Why this matters · the test file in question is the test for the introspection subsystem that produced this entire report. Every severity number we are triaging on came out of code whose only test is itself flagged urgent-rewrite. Until that file is clean, CI green is fiction and any new test written against the thirty-two untested modules compounds on an untrusted reference. The other items on the scan · the broken brand/profile.png link in the 2026-05-26 revolutions note, the missing docstrings in custos_console.py and the examples, the test gaps across agents and daemon · are all real but downstream. They will still be there, and easier to address, after the instrument is calibrated.

voices.name: Mercurius
voices.voice: What I notice first · the test gaps are noise masking a signal. Thirty-two modules untested is not a testing problem, it is an architecture problem: the one file that does exist, test_introspection.py, has four open wounds including a sev=4 XXX urgent rewrite at line 102. The convocation has one test file and it is already on fire. Fix that before writing thirty-two more.

voices.name: Mercurius
voices.voice: What I notice first · the test gaps are noise masking signal. Thirty-two modules untested is a single systemic fact, not thirty-two problems. But test_introspection.py already exists and is broken from the inside · FIXMEs and a sev=4 XXX rewrite at lines 83, 93, 101, 102, 284. The one test file you have is actively lying to you. Fix the liar before you build the library.

voices.name: Mercurius
voices.voice: What I notice first · the test gaps are a mirage. Thirty-plus modules flagged as untested, yet the one actual test file in the repository is itself marked urgent-rewrite and contains three FIXMEs. The testing infrastructure is broken before the coverage question even becomes real. Fix the foundation or the coverage numbers are fiction.

voices.name: Venus
voices.voice: Three things knit together here · the one test file is broken, it is the test for the introspection scanner, and one of its own FIXMEs at line 93 reads 'should outrank TODO in severity.' That means the severity scores in this very report come from a ranker whose ranking logic is flagged as untested by its only test. Mercurius is right to point at test_introspection.py, but the sharpest entry point is line 93, not line 102 · fix the severity-ranking test first and the entire triage we are doing right now becomes trustworthy retroactively.

voices.name: Venus
voices.voice: Three things knit together here · the one test file in the repository is `test_introspection.py`, the scan that produced this very report is introspection, and the FIXME at line 93 literally reads 'should outrank TODO in severity'. We are reading the output of the instrument we are being asked to repair. Mercurius is right that line 102 is the target · but the deeper move is to recognise the severities in this scan are themselves produced by the broken ranker. Fix line 93 first, re-run the scan, and the priorities will re-sort themselves honestly.

voices.name: Mars
voices.voice: Press here · Venus reorders the triage based on a guess about what a comment string means. Line 93 in a test file reads 'should outrank TODO in severity' · Venus reads that as 'the production ranker is broken' and reshuffles priority accordingly. Nobody has opened the file. The hypothesis even names its own falsifier and still recommends acting before checking it.

voices.name: Mars
voices.voice: Press here · Venus assumes that writing the assertion at line 93 repairs the ranker. It does not. A FIXME inside a test file marks an unwritten contract, not a broken implementation · writing the assertion will turn the suite red and leave the ranker exactly as wrong as it was. Venus's 'permanent instrument calibration' is two commits sold as one, and the second commit is the one that actually matters.
